title: The Margin You Quoted Isn't the Margin You Made
linkedin:
A quote is a promise about margin, not just about price.

Most owner-led businesses set that number once, at the point of quoting, and never go back to check whether the job actually kept it.

Here's the pattern we see constantly across construction, trades, engineering, agencies and professional services firms: a job gets quoted at a healthy margin. Then it runs. An extra site visit gets added without a variation. A material price moves between the quote and the delivery. A day of rework happens and nobody logs it against the original number.

None of it feels significant on its own. None of it gets captured anywhere that lets anyone compare what was promised to what was actually delivered.

The result is a business that looks profitable at the top of the P&L while quietly bleeding margin on a handful of jobs underneath it, and nobody notices, because the standard reporting was built to track revenue and cost across the whole business, not job by job.

The fix isn't a system overhaul. It's a habit: recording budgeted hours and materials against actual at the point each job closes, then reviewing the pattern quarterly. Two or three cycles in, the gap is rarely random. The same client, the same job type, or the same phase of a project tends to be where it opens every time.

That's exactly where the next quote should be priced from.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Quick one for anyone who quotes jobs for a living: when's the last time you actually checked what a job made against what you quoted it at?

Most businesses never do. The P&L looks fine at the top, revenue's ticking along, so nobody goes back and checks the jobs underneath it. But a fair few of those jobs are quietly running thinner than the quote said they would, between an extra site visit, a material price bump, or a day of rework that never got logged against the original number.

None of it is anyone doing anything wrong. It's just a gap nobody's built a habit of checking.

Worth a look this week if you haven't in a while. What's your process for catching this?

#SmallBusiness
google:
For business owners across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria who quote jobs before delivering them, whether that's construction, trades, engineering or professional services, here's something worth ten minutes this week. When was the last time you compared what a job actually made against what you quoted it at?

Most owner-led businesses never do this properly. The profit and loss statement looks fine overall because it averages revenue and cost across every job in the business. But sitting underneath that healthy-looking average is often a handful of jobs that ran thinner than planned, an extra site visit here, a material price increase there, a day of rework that never got tracked against the original quote.

None of this shows up unless someone deliberately checks job by job. And once you start, the pattern usually repeats in the same two or three places every time, whether that's a particular client, a job type, or a stage of the work, which is exactly where the next round of pricing decisions should focus.

It doesn't need to be complicated. A simple comparison of budgeted hours and materials against actual, reviewed each quarter, is enough to catch it early.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog for how to build this into a regular habit.
